photos.title: First 90cm Round at Spring Valley
photos.description: Star locking onto the first oxer of our 90cm round at Spring Valley. I was nervous, but she took me to the fence like a pro.

photos.title: Tight Turn to the Double
photos.description: Second fence of the tricky two-stride double at Spring Valley. We practiced this turn for weeks at home.

photos.title: Clearing the Water Tray
photos.description: Our first clear jump over a spooky water tray at the county show. Star hesitated for a stride, then flew.

photos.title: First Red Ribbon Finish
photos.description: Walking out of the arena after our first red ribbon in the 80cm class. I could not stop smiling.

photos.title: Evening Jump-Off Round
photos.description: Our fastest round of the season under the evening lights. Star felt light and focused over every fence.

photos.title: Waiting at the In-Gate
photos.description: One last pat and a deep breath while we wait for the bell. Star watches the last rider leave the arena.

photos.title: Final Fence of the Season
photos.description: The last fence of our 2022 competition season. We finished on a clear round and a huge boost of confidence.

photos.title: Prizegiving Lap of Honor
photos.description: Star cantering the lap of honor with her ribbon flapping on the bridle. She clearly enjoyed the applause.

photos.title: First 1m Class Warm-Up
photos.description: Stretching over a warm-up oxer before our first 1m class. A big milestone for both of us.

photos.title: Checking the Course Map
photos.description: Studying the jumpoff course with Star half-dozing beside me. The calm before the adrenaline.

photos.title: Gridwork in the Outdoor Arena
photos.description: A line of placing poles and small crosses to help Star find her rhythm and improve her technique over fences.

photos.title: Canter Poles for Suppleness
photos.description: Using raised canter poles on a circle to encourage Star to sit and balance in the corners.

photos.title: First Attempt at Bow
photos.description: Star learning to stretch one leg forward and lower her head for the bow trick, rewarded with lots of scratches.

photos.title: Golden Hour Portrait
photos.description: Star standing quietly in the field at sunset, ears pricked to the sound of dinner arriving.

photos.title: Morning Feed Time
photos.description: Star waiting patiently with her head over the stable door as I mix her morning feed.

photos.title: Post-Ride Cool Down
photos.description: Walking Star out after a schooling session, letting her stretch and relax before heading back to the stable.

polls.description: Help me choose Star’s next trick to work on in our clicker training sessions. Options include Bow, Spanish walk, Lie down, and Kiss.

articles.title: Building Star’s Winter Jumping Base with Low Grids
articles.summary: How I used simple low grids over winter to rebuild Star’s confidence, balance, and strength before the 2024 show season.
articles.content:
Building Star’s Winter Jumping Base with Low Grids

This winter I focused on low, confidence-building grids instead of big fences. Star had a lighter competition schedule in 2023, so I wanted to quietly rebuild her strength and enthusiasm.

The basic grid setup

I set up three cross-poles with placing poles before and after the line. The distances were short enough to encourage a bouncy, round canter rather than speed.

Goals for each session

  • Rhythm through the grid in both directions
  • Even, straight approach and departure
  • Soft, elastic contact instead of pulling to the jumps

By keeping the fences small, I could focus on my position and Star could focus on her technique. This laid the groundwork for the bigger “Jumping” sessions you’ll see in later 2024 training posts.

articles.title: Gymnastic Lines to Sharpen Our 90cm Rounds
articles.summary: A look at the gymnastic lines I used in early 2024 to help Star stay careful and rideable around 90cm courses.
articles.content:
Gymnastic Lines to Sharpen Our 90cm Rounds

With Star feeling stronger after our winter gridwork, I started adding slightly more challenging gymnastic lines in February 2024.

The 90cm exercise

The line was a vertical to oxer combination, followed by three strides to another vertical. Everything stayed around 80–90cm, but the focus was on control, not height.

What I looked for

  • A steady approach to the first fence
  • Quick, thoughtful hind legs through the line
  • Soft downward transition after the last vertical

These jumping sessions made a huge difference once we started walking 2024 show courses and riding our first rounds of the season.

articles.title: First Outdoor Course Walk of 2024
articles.summary: How our first full outdoor jumping course of 2024 went, plus the lines I set to mimic early-season shows.
articles.content:
First Outdoor Course Walk of 2024

As soon as the ground dried enough, I set a full course outside to see how well our winter jumping homework had worked.

Course design

I built a 10-fence course with a mix of oxers, verticals, a related distance, and a small two-stride combination. Heights stayed at 85–90cm to keep it confidence building.

Takeaways

  1. Star stayed in a consistent rhythm between fences.
  2. Our turns to the related distance still need work.
  3. The winter focus on grids and gymnastic lines really paid off.

This session became the foundation for several more 2024 jumping training posts you’ll see under the Training & Jumping filters.

articles.title: Flatwork Day: Suppling Before Our Jump Schools
articles.summary: The simple flatwork routine I ride the day before a big jumping school to keep Star loose and attentive.
articles.content:
Flatwork Day: Suppling Before Our Jump Schools

Good jumping starts with good flatwork. The day before I school bigger fences, I ride a lighter session focused on suppleness and relaxation.

We use lots of circles, transitions, and some lateral work to check in with Star’s balance and response to my aids.

articles.title: Teaching Star Her First Trick: Bow
articles.summary: Step-by-step notes from our first sessions teaching Star to bow using clicker training and lots of patience.
articles.content:
Teaching Star Her First Trick: Bow

I’ve always wanted to teach Star a few tricks, so we started with a simple but elegant one: the bow. It fits her personality and doesn’t put too much strain on her body when done correctly.

Our clicker training setup

I used a target, a clicker, and small high-value treats. The goal in the first few sessions was simply to reward any stretch of her neck toward the ground.

Progress so far

By the end of the week, Star understood that stretching down and forward with one front leg slightly advanced earned a reward. We’re still refining the duration of the bow and building the cue.

In the sidebar poll on this page, you can vote on which trick we should work on next after bow. I’d love to hear your thoughts in the comments as well.

articles.title: Canter Poles on a Circle for Balance
articles.summary: A simple canter pole exercise that helped Star stay soft and adjustable through the turns.
articles.content:
Canter Poles on a Circle for Balance

This exercise uses four poles on a 20m circle to encourage balance, rhythm, and adjustability in the canter.

It became a staple in our program before stepping up Star’s jumping height in later seasons.

articles.title: What Star’s Right Hind Lameness Taught Me
articles.summary: A mild lameness episode in 2024 forced us to slow down and rethink Star’s workload, warm-up routine, and recovery days.
articles.content:
What Star’s Right Hind Lameness Taught Me

In early summer 2024, Star came out of her stable slightly short on the right hind. It was subtle, but enough to make me cancel our training ride and call the vet.

We discovered a mild strain, likely from a playful moment in the paddock. The experience made me reconsider how I balance training intensity, rest days, and footing conditions.

articles.title: Managing Mild Lameness After a Paddock Slip
articles.summary: The step-by-step plan we followed in 2026 after Star slipped in the paddock and came up mildly lame on her left fore.
articles.content:
Managing Mild Lameness After a Paddock Slip

On a frosty January morning, Star did an enthusiastic spin in the paddock and slipped. She walked away sound at first, but an hour later she was clearly short on the left fore.

I immediately called the vet, iced the leg, and kept her in a small pen until we had a clear diagnosis. This article walks through the timeline from the first signs of lameness, to the vet visit, to her gradual return to work.

If you’re reading this after dealing with your own lameness scare, you are not alone. I hope our experience and the vet’s advice give you a framework for talking to your own team.

articles.title: Hoof Care Routine for a Jumping Horse
articles.summary: How we schedule farrier visits, daily picking, and after-ride care to keep Star’s feet healthy for jumping.
articles.content:
Hoof Care Routine for a Jumping Horse

Good feet are non-negotiable for a jumping horse. In this post I share our farrier schedule, daily hoof care, and how we manage mud season to avoid issues.

articles.title: Fine-Tuning Star’s Oats Feed Before Summer Competitions
articles.summary: Why I adjusted Star’s oats feed and overall ration ahead of a busy 2025 summer show schedule.
articles.content:
Fine-Tuning Star’s Oats Feed Before Summer Competitions

As we headed into the 2025 summer season, I realized Star needed a little more energy without becoming sharp. Instead of overhauling her entire ration, I made small, careful adjustments to her oats feed and forage balance.

In this article I explain the exact changes, how I monitored her weight and attitude, and when I backed off the oats during quieter weeks.

If you are considering changing your own horse’s oats or grain, please speak with a vet or nutritionist who knows your situation.

articles.title: Review: Our New Close-Contact Jump Saddle
articles.summary: Why I finally upgraded Star’s jump saddle in 2024, what I chose, and how it’s changed my position over fences.
articles.content:
Review: Our New Close-Contact Jump Saddle

After several seasons in a well-loved but older saddle, I decided it was time to invest in something that fitted both Star and me more precisely.

In this review I cover the fitting process, what I liked and didn’t like about different models, and how the final choice has felt in training and at competitions.

articles.title: Front Boots for Jumping: What Worked for Star
articles.summary: Comparing different front boots we tried for Star and which ones held up best in training and shows.
articles.content:
Front Boots for Jumping: What Worked for Star

Over the last few seasons we tried several styles of front boots for Star. Some rubbed, some slipped, and a few became instant favorites.

This post compares fit, protection, and durability so you can see what might work for your own horse.

articles.title: First Show of 2023: Finding Our Rhythm Again
articles.summary: A recap of our first 2023 competition, including a wobbly warm-up, a brave round, and what I want to improve next time.
articles.content:
First Show of 2023: Finding Our Rhythm Again

Our first competition of 2023 felt a bit rusty. Star was keen, I was nervous, and our distances were not always perfect.

Despite a few deep spots in the 80cm class, she jumped her heart out. This post goes through each round, what worked, and what I changed in training afterward.

articles.title: Mid-Season High: Our First 90cm Clear Round
articles.summary: How Star and I finally put together a smooth, clear 90cm round halfway through the 2023 season.
articles.content:
Mid-Season High: Our First 90cm Clear Round

By June 2023, all the flatwork and gymnastic training started to show. At a local show, Star and I jumped our first clear 90cm round.

The lines rode forward, the turns felt balanced, and I finished the course with a huge grin on my face.

articles.title: 2022 Season Finale: Ending on a Clear Round
articles.summary: A look back at our final show of 2022, where Star finished the season with a confident clear.
articles.content:
2022 Season Finale: Ending on a Clear Round

The last show of 2022 felt like the perfect wrap-up to our year. Star jumped a calm, rhythmic round and finished with a lovely clear.

We didn’t chase ribbons that day; we just wanted a confident, happy experience in the ring—and we got it.

articles.title: Stepping Up: Our First 1m Class
articles.summary: All the nerves and excitement from moving Star up to her first 1m class in 2024.
articles.content:
Stepping Up: Our First 1m Class

In April 2024 we finally entered a 1m class. The fences definitely looked bigger, but all the homework from our Training posts helped us feel ready.

We had one rail down from a slightly deep spot, but overall I was thrilled with how confidently Star jumped.

poll_options.label: Adjust feed weekly based on workload
Adjust feed monthly or with each new plan
Only change feed for big workload changes
Rarely change feed once a routine is set
